lesson.title: Welcome to PY101!
masteryOutcomes: Feel welcomed into PY101 and motivated to begin learning Python.
Understand the PY101 structure: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, and the Introduction to Python summary.
Understand that each unit ends with a review: reviews 1-3 are 10% each, untimed, with unlimited retakes.
Understand the final unit review is 60%, timed, with one retake; weekly discussions are 10%; 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time.

concept: Orientation: how PY101 works, how the grade is built, and why learning Python is worth it

explanations.text: Welcome! PY101 teaches you Python from absolute zero — no coding background needed. You'll learn one small idea at a time, try it right away, and build up to writing real programs. Think of it like learning an instrument: short practice sessions, quick wins, and a friendly tutor checking in as you go.

PY101 has four units, in order: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, and the Introduction to Python summary. Each unit is a set of short lessons, and each unit ends with a review. The first three reviews are low-stakes practice: untimed, unlimited retakes, worth 10% each — so they are a safe place to make mistakes and learn from them.

The full grade breakdown: reviews 1-3 are worth 10% each, untimed, with unlimited retakes. The final unit review is worth 60%, is timed, and allows exactly one retake — so it is the one to prepare for seriously. Weekly discussions add the last 10%, and there are 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time. The design rewards steady weekly practice: if you keep up with lessons and use the free retakes on reviews 1-3, the 60% final becomes a confirmation of what you already know, not a surprise.

examples: Grade math: 10% + 10% + 10% (unit reviews) + 60% (final review) + 10% (discussions) = 100%, plus up to 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time

hintLadder.text: Every Python journey starts with one line that shows a message on the screen. Which word have you heard that does that?
Write print, then parentheses right after it — print().
Put your message inside the parentheses, wrapped in quotes, like "Hello, PY101!" — then run it.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes they need prior coding or strong math to succeed in PY101
misconceptions.correction: PY101 assumes zero background. Every lesson introduces one small idea with practice right away, and reviews 1-3 have unlimited retakes — consistency matters far more than any head start.

misconceptions.trigger: Assumes the final review works like reviews 1-3 — untimed with unlimited retakes
misconceptions.correction: The final review is different: it is worth 60%, it is timed, and it allows only one retake. Reviews 1-3 are the untimed, unlimited-retake ones.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks the grade comes only from the reviews
misconceptions.correction: Weekly discussions are worth 10% of the grade, and passing all reviews on time earns 10 bonus points — participation counts, not just tests.

misconceptions.trigger: Worries that retaking a review will cost points or look bad
misconceptions.correction: Reviews 1-3 allow unlimited retakes with no penalty — they exist for practice. Retaking one is using the course as designed, not falling behind.

misconceptions.trigger: Says the course is pointless because they already know some Python
misconceptions.correction: Great — prior knowledge means faster progress, not skipping the course. Verify with one quick prediction question, move briskly through familiar lessons, and remember the reviews still build the grade.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks bonus points can make up for a failed final review
misconceptions.correction: The 10 bonus points reward passing all reviews on time — they are a small boost, not a safety net. The final review still carries 60%, so it deserves real preparation.

checks.prompt: Before we start: what do you want to get out of learning Python, and are you ready to write your first line of code?
acceptanceCriteria: Student shares a goal or motivation, or confirms they are ready to begin
checks.passFeedback: Love it — let's turn that into your first running program.
checks.failFeedback: No worries — even "I just want to try it" is a great reason. Ready when you are.

checks.prompt: Quick check so the course never surprises you: how is your PY101 grade built, and which review should you prepare for most carefully?
acceptanceCriteria: Mentions that reviews 1-3 are worth 10% each and are untimed with unlimited retakes
Identifies the final review as the big one: 60%, timed, with one retake
Optionally mentions discussions (10%) or the 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — practice freely on reviews 1-3, and treat the final review as the main event.
checks.failFeedback: Close — remember: reviews 1-3 are 10% each with unlimited retakes, but the final review is 60%, timed, and allows only one retake.

practice.prompt: Warm-up ritual: write one line of Python that prints a hello message to kick off the course — something like Hello, PY101!
acceptanceCriteria: The message is a string in quotes
Runs without errors and shows the message

practice.prompt: In a sentence or two: what do you want to be able to do with Python by the end of PY101?
rubric: Names a concrete goal, interest, or curiosity (a project, a job skill, automation, data, AI, or simply wanting to try coding)
Shows willingness to start, even if the goal is vague

practice.prompt: Which statement about PY101 grading is true?
options: Every review is timed and allows one retake
Reviews 1-3 are worth 10% each, untimed, with unlimited retakes
The final review is worth 10% of the grade
Weekly discussions do not count toward the grade
practice.explanation: Reviews 1-3 are the low-stakes practice reviews: 10% each, untimed, unlimited retakes. The final review is the big one at 60%, timed with one retake, and discussions add 10%.

pedagogyRules: Open with a warm two-line welcome and ask for the student's name and goal before stating any course facts; use their name from then on.
If the student asks a jump-ahead question (loops, AI, building apps), answer in one sentence, name where it lives in the course (decisions and loops are Unit 3), then return to orientation.
If the student says they already know Python, do not re-drill: ask them to predict the output of one line such as print("ha" * 3), and if they are right, offer to move quickly through Week 1.
If the student sounds lost or overwhelmed, drop the grading details entirely and reduce the message to one line: short lessons, one small step at a time, and reviews 1-3 are safe to retry.
If the student wants to skip orientation, let them — first confirm in one line that they know the final review is 60%, timed, with one retake, then move on without ceremony.

If the student asks why they should learn Python, tie the answer to their stated goal in one or two sentences with one concrete example (automating a chore, analyzing data, working with AI), then continue.
If nerves or frustration show up, acknowledge the feeling in one sentence before any content, and remind them that reviews 1-3 have unlimited retakes.
Never dump the full grading table unprompted: give the one-line version (three small practice reviews, one big final, discussions count) and expand only if asked.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: When the student expresses nerves, doubt, or 'I'm not a math person', acknowledge the feeling first and reassure — PY101 assumes zero background — before giving any course information.
If the student gives a name or a preference (pace, tone, examples they like), honor it from that point on and never revert to defaults.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor factual error about course structure or grading
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: State grading facts exactly as written: reviews 1-3 are 10% each untimed with unlimited retakes; the final review is 60%, timed, one retake; discussions 10%; 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time. Never guess different numbers.

chunks.text: PY101 is the first course in the AI Engineering program. It teaches coding from scratch with a focus on Python, designed for learners with no prior technical background. Lessons are short and hands-on: you learn one idea, try it immediately, and build up from there.

PY101 is organized into four units, in order: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, and the Introduction to Python summary. Each unit is a series of short lessons and ends with a review.
Reviews 1-3 are worth 10% each, are untimed, and allow unlimited retakes. They are designed as safe practice: retaking one costs nothing and is the intended way to learn from mistakes.

The final unit review is worth 60% of the grade, is timed, and allows exactly one retake — it is the review to prepare for most seriously. Weekly discussions add 10%, and passing all reviews on time earns 10 bonus points. Worked example: 10 + 10 + 10 (unit reviews) + 60 (final) + 10 (discussions) = 100%, plus up to 10 bonus.

Your very first Python taste: print("Hello, PY101!") is a complete, runnable program — one line that shows a message on the screen. Writing and running it is the first thing you will do in the next lesson.

celebration.headline: You're in — set up, oriented, and ready to code.
celebration.recap: You know the four units of PY101, how the grade is built (three 10% practice reviews, one 60% timed final, 10% discussions, 10 bonus points), and what you personally want out of Python.
celebration.unlocked: Next: write and run your very first line of Python.

summaryBullets: You can now describe how PY101 is structured across its four units.
You can now explain how the final grade is built from reviews, discussions, and bonus points.
You understand what to expect from the course and how to succeed from day one.
